methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $370M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $700M - $3.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: KoBold Metals
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $2.96B

private.name: Electric Hydrogen
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $1B

private.name: Amogy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $700M

data.narrative:
Boston Metal, a Series C climate and energy company founded by Sadoway and Allanore, is valued here against a peer universe of three same-stage private comparables in the Climate & Energy sector (KoBold Metals at $3.0B, Electric Hydrogen at $1.0B, and Amogy at $700M), supplemented by a broader set of eight private peers and one public comparable (Linamar, LNZA).

Two valuation methods triangulate to a coherent range. The Stage-Progression method applies a 1.7x step-up multiplier to Boston Metal's $370M in total raised, implying a valuation of $629M. The Peer-Median approach anchors to the three same-sector, same-stage comparables, which exhibit a median valuation of $1.0B with a range of $700M to $3.0B. This wide dispersion reflects heterogeneity in technology maturity and commercialization progress across the peer set.

Synthesis across both methods yields a low estimate of $535M, a median of $814M, and a high of $1.1B. The Stage-Progression method supports the lower end of this range, while peer multiples push toward the upper bound. Key uncertainty lies in the significant spread among peers—a 4.3x gap between Amogy and KoBold—which reflects the early-stage, capital-intensive nature of frontier energy technologies. Technology differentiation, pathway to unit economics, and customer offtake commitments remain critical variables not captured in static valuation multiples.
